---
title: "Curriculum Roadmap"
subtitle: "Every lesson, why it's here, and the interview questions it prepares you for — from base rates to foundation models in biology."
---

::: {.specimen}
**The one message of this book**

Real data does not arrive labeled with the method that solves it. The single
most valuable skill in applied biostatistics is **pattern-matching a messy
problem to the right family of solutions** — recognizing "this is a base-rate
problem," "this is a maximum-likelihood problem," "this sequence has memory, so
think Markov," "there's no closed form, so simulate it." Every lesson here is
built to sharpen that reflex. If you finish a lesson able to *recognize the
problem type in the wild*, it did its job.
:::

This is a curriculum in **statistics and machine learning for bioinformatics**,
built to do three jobs at once: teach the concepts rigorously, prepare you for
hard technical interviews at top companies and labs, and stay modern — the
examples come from single-cell sequencing, neural networks, and large language
models, not coin flips and urns.

It is organized as six tracks. Earlier tracks are prerequisites for later ones
in spirit if not in strict order. Each lesson lists its status, its core ideas,
and — because that is half the point of this site — **the interview questions it
unlocks**.

::: {.lens}
The interview questions throughout are written in the spirit of a technical
interview at a top research group or company: less "state the formula," more
"here is a messy dataset — what do you do, what do you assume, and how would you
know if you were wrong?" They double as a self-test. If you can answer them out
loud, you know the lesson.
:::

::: {.callout-note}
## Who this is for
- **Graduate students** learning statistics and bioinformatics who want
  intuition that survives past the exam.
- **Late-stage PhDs and postdocs** preparing for demanding technical interviews.
- **Working professionals** who need a fast, trustworthy refresher on a key
  concept or workflow.

A recurring thread — **single-cell RNA-seq** — runs through the tracks as a
shared worked example, so concepts compound instead of resetting each lesson.
:::

# Track A · Inference foundations {#track-a}

The statistics that every quantitative interview assumes you own. If you can
only study one track before an interview, study this one.

### Lesson 1 — Bayesian Statistics · *live* {#lesson-01}

[Open the lesson →](lessons/01-bayesian-statistics/index.qmd)

Updating beliefs with evidence: priors, likelihoods, posteriors, and why base
rates are the most-ignored number in science. Natural-frequency reasoning, the
odds form, and where priors come from.

**Reach for this when:** you have a prior belief and new evidence, or when a
"detector" result needs to be reconciled with how rare the thing is.

**Interview questions this unlocks**

- A test is "99% accurate" and you test positive. Why isn't your answer 99%?
- What is a prior, and what do you do when you don't have a good one?
- Explain the base-rate fallacy to a collaborator who just p-hacked a "discovery."

### Lesson 2 — *p*-values & Hypothesis Testing · *planned* {#lesson-02}

What a *p*-value is, what it is emphatically *not*, and the machinery of the
null-hypothesis significance test. Type I and Type II error, the meaning of
$\alpha$, and why "not significant" is not "no effect" [@wasserstein2016].

**Reach for this when:** you need to ask whether an observed effect could
plausibly be noise.

**Interview questions this unlocks**

- Define a *p*-value in one sentence. Now list two things it is *not*.
- You ran 40 tests and one came back at $p = 0.04$. Should you be excited?
- What's the difference between statistical and practical significance?

### Lesson 3 — *t*-tests & the *t*-distribution · *planned* {#lesson-03}

One-sample, two-sample, and paired *t*-tests; where the *t*-distribution comes
from and why it has fatter tails than the normal; the assumptions and what to do
when they fail — including when to reach for a non-parametric test.

**Reach for this when:** you're comparing the means of one or two groups with
limited samples.

**Interview questions this unlocks**

- When would you use a paired *t*-test instead of a two-sample test?
- Your two groups have wildly different variances. Now what?
- Why *t* and not *z*? What is a degree of freedom, intuitively?

### Lesson 4 — Confidence Intervals & the Bootstrap · *planned* {#lesson-04}

What a confidence interval actually claims (and the interpretation everyone gets
wrong), the duality with hypothesis tests, and the bootstrap as a way to get an
interval when the formula is intractable — your first taste of solving a problem
by *resampling* rather than deriving.

**Reach for this when:** you need an uncertainty range, especially when no clean
formula exists.

**Interview questions this unlocks**

- A 95% CI is [2, 8]. What does "95%" refer to — and what does it *not*?
- Explain the bootstrap to someone who's never seen it.
- When does a confidence interval mislead you?

### Lesson 5 — Maximum Likelihood Estimation · *planned* {#lesson-05}

The estimation engine under almost every model in this book. The likelihood as
"how well do these parameters explain the data," why we maximize its log, and
the deep idea that **fitting a model = optimizing a likelihood**. MLE vs. MAP
vs. full Bayesian (the through-line back to Lesson 1); standard errors from
curvature; and the link forward to gradient descent
([Lesson 17](#lesson-17)) as *how* the maximization actually happens.

**Reach for this when:** you need to estimate the parameters of a model you can
write down a probability for — which is most of them.

**Interview questions this unlocks**

- What is a likelihood, and how is it different from a probability?
- Derive the MLE for a coin's bias. Why maximize the *log*-likelihood?
- Logistic regression, GLMs, and neural nets are all doing the same thing under
  the hood. What is it?
- How does MLE relate to MAP and to the Bayesian posterior?

### Lesson 6 — Multiple Comparisons & FDR · *planned* {#lesson-06}

The problem that defines modern genomics: run 20,000 differential-expression
tests and thousands come back "significant" by chance. Family-wise error vs.
false discovery rate; Bonferroni, Benjamini–Hochberg, and *q*-values
[@benjamini1995; @storey2003]. Worked on a single-cell differential-expression
example.

**Reach for this when:** you're running many tests at once (any -omics screen).

**Interview questions this unlocks**

- You tested 20,000 genes at $\alpha = 0.05$. How many false positives under the
  null? What do you do about it?
- Bonferroni vs. Benjamini–Hochberg — when and why?
- What is a *q*-value, and how is it different from a *p*-value?

### Lesson 7 — Statistical Power & Experimental Design · *planned* {#lesson-07}

Effect size, sample size, and the four-way relationship between $\alpha$, power,
effect size, and $n$. Why underpowered studies are worse than useless, and the
special headaches of powering a single-cell experiment (cells vs. samples — the
pseudo-replication trap).

**Reach for this when:** you're designing a study and need to know if it *can*
answer its question before you run it.

**Interview questions this unlocks**

- Design a power analysis for an experiment I describe. What do you need to know?
- Why is an underpowered "significant" result especially untrustworthy?
- In scRNA-seq, is your sample size the number of cells or the number of animals?

# Track B · Data transformation & normalization {#track-b}

### Lesson 8 — Normalization & Standardization · *planned* {#lesson-08}

The transformations you apply before almost any analysis, and the ones
interviewers love because everyone fumbles them. Z-scores, min–max, log/log1p,
variance-stabilizing transforms (rlog, VST), CPM/TPM, size-factor and
median-of-ratios normalization, `SCTransform`, and quantile normalization —
framed on bulk **and single-cell** RNA-seq. When each is right, and what each
quietly distorts.

**Reach for this when:** raw measurements aren't comparable across samples,
features, or sequencing depth — i.e. always, first.

**Interview questions this unlocks**

- You have scRNA-seq counts with wildly different depth per cell. Normalize it —
  and justify every step.
- When is a z-score the wrong choice? What does it assume?
- Why log-transform? What breaks at zero (and why `log1p`)?
- Quantile normalization forces a shared distribution. Feature or lie?

# Track C · Regression & statistical models {#track-c}

### Lesson 9 — Linear Regression · *planned* {#lesson-09}

The workhorse. What the coefficients mean, the assumptions, and — crucially —
the diagnostic plots that tell you when it's lying to you.

**Reach for this when:** you want to model a continuous outcome as a function of
predictors, or just need an interpretable baseline.

**Interview questions this unlocks**

- Interpret this coefficient. Now interpret it if I add a correlated predictor.
- I show you a residual-vs-fitted plot. What are you looking for?
- What does $R^2$ tell you, and what does it hide?

### Lesson 10 — Logistic Regression & GLMs · *planned* {#lesson-10}

From lines to log-odds — and a concrete payoff of maximum likelihood
([Lesson 5](#lesson-05)). Link functions, coefficients as odds ratios, the GLM
family (including the negative-binomial models behind RNA-seq differential
expression), and calibration.

**Reach for this when:** your outcome is a category, a count, or a rate rather
than a continuous number.

**Interview questions this unlocks**

- Why can't you just use linear regression for a 0/1 outcome?
- Interpret a logistic coefficient of 0.7.
- Why is RNA-seq count data modeled with a negative binomial and not a Poisson?

### Lesson 11 — Regularization & the Bias–Variance Tradeoff · *planned* {#lesson-11}

The central tension of all model-fitting, and the conceptual bridge from
statistics to machine learning. Overfitting and underfitting made visual; ridge
vs. lasso; cross-validation as the honest estimate of out-of-sample error.

**Reach for this when:** you have many predictors, few samples, or a model that
memorizes instead of generalizes (the *p ≫ n* world of genomics).

**Interview questions this unlocks**

- Draw the bias–variance tradeoff. Where does overfitting live?
- Ridge vs. lasso — what's the difference and when do you reach for each?
- Why do we need a validation set separate from the test set?

# Track D · Probabilistic & computational modeling {#track-d}

When the world has *dependence over time or sequence*, or when a problem has no
clean closed-form answer, these are the two reflexes that save you. This track
is the purest expression of the book's message: recognizing the *shape* of a
problem tells you which machine to build.

### Lesson 12 — Monte Carlo Methods & Simulation · *planned* {#lesson-12}

The "if you can't derive it, simulate it" superpower. Estimating quantities by
random sampling; permutation and randomization tests (the assumption-light way
to get a *p*-value); simulating null distributions and statistical power; and an
intuitive introduction to **Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC)** as the engine that
makes Bayesian inference ([Lesson 1](#lesson-01)) computable. The bootstrap
([Lesson 4](#lesson-04)) is revealed as a Monte Carlo method all along.

**Reach for this when:** the math is intractable, assumptions are shaky, or you
can describe a data-generating process but not solve it — simulate and count.

**Interview questions this unlocks**

- Your test's assumptions are violated. How do you get a *p*-value anyway?
- Explain a permutation test. Why is it so trustworthy?
- What problem does MCMC solve, and why can't we just compute the posterior
  directly?
- How would you estimate the power of a complicated study design with no formula?

### Lesson 13 — Markov Chains & Hidden Markov Models · *planned* {#lesson-13}

Modeling sequences with memory — one of the most important tool-families in all
of bioinformatics. Markov chains and the memoryless property; **hidden** Markov
models when the state you care about is unobserved; and the algorithms that make
them usable — Viterbi (most likely path) and forward–backward. Grounded in the
canonical biological applications: gene finding, CpG-island detection, profile
HMMs for sequence families, and base-calling.

**Reach for this when:** your data is an ordered sequence (DNA, protein, time)
and the next element depends on the current hidden state.

**Interview questions this unlocks**

- What is the Markov property, and when is it a reasonable assumption for
  biological sequence data?
- What makes an HMM "hidden"? Give a biological example of the hidden states.
- At a high level, what do the Viterbi and forward–backward algorithms each
  compute, and when would you use which?
- How is a profile HMM different from a simple sequence alignment?

# Track E · Machine learning: workflow to neural networks {#track-e}

The interview core for any ML-for-science role. Heavy on **decision
flowcharts** — the point is the *process* and the *intuition for how models
learn*, not memorized definitions.

### Lesson 14 — The ML Workflow · *planned* {#lesson-14}

How to actually approach an ML problem, start to finish, as decision flowcharts:
is this even an ML problem? Supervised or unsupervised? How do you split the
data? What's your baseline? What statistics do you capture and what plots do you
generate at each stage? Includes hands-on "train a tiny model" walkthroughs.

**Reach for this when:** someone hands you data and a vague goal and you need a
principled first move.

**Interview questions this unlocks**

- I hand you a dataset and a vague goal. Walk me through your first hour.
- What's your baseline, and why do you always build one first?
- What would you plot after training, before trusting anything?

### Lesson 15 — Model Evaluation & Selection · *planned* {#lesson-15}

The metrics and the traps. Train/validation/test discipline, cross-validation,
confusion matrices, ROC vs. precision–recall, class imbalance (ubiquitous in
biology), and data leakage — the silent killer of real projects.

**Reach for this when:** you have a model and need to know whether to trust it,
and which of several to ship.

**Interview questions this unlocks**

- Accuracy is 99% and the model is useless. How is that possible?
- ROC-AUC vs. PR-AUC — which do you report on a rare-event problem, and why?
- Define data leakage and give me three ways it sneaks into a genomics pipeline.
- Your CV score is great and production performance is terrible. Debug it.

### Lesson 16 — Unsupervised Learning & Dimensionality Reduction · *planned* {#lesson-16}

Structure without labels — the heart of single-cell analysis. PCA, k-means and
graph-based (Leiden/Louvain) clustering, and UMAP/t-SNE for visualization — with
a hard focus on how to *not* over-interpret them. The lesson that demystifies the
scRNA-seq UMAP everyone shows and few can defend.

**Reach for this when:** you have unlabeled high-dimensional data and want to
find or visualize structure.

**Interview questions this unlocks**

- What do the axes of a PCA plot mean? What about a UMAP plot? (Trick question.)
- How do you choose the number of clusters, and why is that partly a lie?
- I see three clusters in a UMAP. How do I know they're real cell types and not
  an artifact of the parameters?

### Lesson 17 — Gradient Descent & Optimization · *planned* {#lesson-17}

The engine under nearly every fitted model, and the *how* behind maximum
likelihood ([Lesson 5](#lesson-05)). Learning a parameter by following a
gradient downhill; loss surfaces, learning rate, stochastic vs. batch gradient
descent, and the failure modes (local minima, vanishing/exploding gradients) —
built up visually and interactively, no heavy calculus required.

**Reach for this when:** you need to fit a model with no closed-form solution —
which is essentially all of modern ML.

**Interview questions this unlocks**

- Explain gradient descent to me as if I were a first-year grad student.
- Your loss is oscillating and won't converge. What's your first suspect?
- What is a learning rate, and what happens if it's too big or too small?
- Why *stochastic* gradient descent — what does the randomness buy you?

### Lesson 18 — Neural Networks · *planned* {#lesson-18}

From logistic regression to deep learning, one honest step at a time. A neuron
as a weighted sum plus a nonlinearity; why depth and nonlinearity buy
expressiveness; forward and backward passes (backprop as the chain rule doing
bookkeeping); activations, overfitting, and regularization (dropout, early
stopping). Grounded in a biological classification example.

**Reach for this when:** the relationship is complex and nonlinear and you have
enough data to justify the flexibility.

**Interview questions this unlocks**

- Walk me from logistic regression to a neural network. What's actually new?
- Why do we need nonlinear activation functions at all?
- What is backpropagation, in one paragraph, without the matrix algebra?
- Your network memorizes the training data. Name three ways to fix it.

# Track F · LLMs & modern AI in biology {#track-f}

The destination. Enough intuition for a working scientist — not a
mathematician — to use language and foundation models as instruments for finding
patterns in biological data and text, and to know when *not* to trust them.

### Lesson 19 — Embeddings & Similarity · *planned* {#lesson-19}

The idea that meaning — of a word, a protein, a cell — can be a direction in
space. Vector embeddings, cosine similarity, and why "nearby in embedding space"
is the engine under semantic search, clustering, and single-cell integration.

**Reach for this when:** you want to compare things by *meaning* rather than
exact match — documents, sequences, or cells.

**Interview questions this unlocks**

- What *is* an embedding? What does distance in that space mean?
- Cosine similarity vs. Euclidean distance — when does the choice matter?
- How would you cluster 10,000 abstracts — or 10,000 cells — without labels?

### Lesson 20 — What a Language Model Actually Learns · *planned* {#lesson-20}

Demystifying the machine. Tokens, the next-token objective, attention and
transformers at an intuitive level, and what "learning" means for a model that
only ever predicts the next piece of a sequence — whether English or amino acids.

**Reach for this when:** you need to reason about what an LLM can and can't be
trusted to do before you build on it.

**Interview questions this unlocks**

- In one paragraph, what does training a language model actually optimize?
- Why does next-token prediction produce something that looks like reasoning?
- What can a model trained this way *not* be trusted to do?

### Lesson 21 — LLMs & Foundation Models in Biology · *planned* {#lesson-21}

Putting it to work, responsibly. Protein language models (ESM-style) and
single-cell foundation models; retrieval over a literature corpus; LLMs as noisy
annotators; building simple evals; and the ever-present risks — hallucination,
miscalibration, batch effects masquerading as biology, and confirmation bias
dressed up as discovery.

**Reach for this when:** you want to mine patterns from large biological text or
sequence corpora — and need to prove the results are real.

**Interview questions this unlocks**

- Use an LLM to screen 50,000 abstracts for a phenotype. How do you set it up
  and validate it?
- What does a protein language model embedding capture, and how would you
  sanity-check it?
- The model is confidently wrong 5% of the time. How does that change your
  pipeline design?
- How do you build an eval for a task with no ground-truth labels?

---

::: {.callout-tip}
## This roadmap is a living document
Lessons ship as they're written; the outlines above are firm enough to study
from and will be filled in over time. Track A is the priority, and Lesson 2
(*p*-values) is next. If you're preparing for a specific interview and want a
lesson pulled forward, that's exactly the kind of thing this roadmap exists to
make visible.
:::
